**New Zealand**[[a]](#cite_note-22) is an [island country](/wiki/Island_country "Island country") in the southwestern [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). It consists of two main landmasses—the [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island") (*Te Ika-a-Māui*) and the [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island") (*Te Waipounamu*)—and [over 600 smaller islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_New_Zealand "List of islands of New Zealand"). It is the [sixth-largest island country](/wiki/List_of_island_countries "List of island countries") by area and lies east of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") across the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea") and south of the islands of [New Caledonia](/wiki/New_Caledonia "New Caledonia"), [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), and [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga"). The [country's varied topography](/wiki/Geography_of_New_Zealand "Geography of New Zealand") and sharp mountain peaks, including the [Southern Alps](/wiki/Southern_Alps "Southern Alps") (*Kā Tiritiri o te Moana*), owe much to [tectonic uplift](/wiki/Tectonic_uplift "Tectonic uplift") and volcanic eruptions. [New Zealand's capital city](/wiki/Capital_of_New_Zealand "Capital of New Zealand") is [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington"), and its most populous city is [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland").

The islands of New Zealand were the last large habitable land to be settled by humans. Between about 1280 and 1350, [Polynesians](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") began to settle in the islands and subsequently developed a distinctive [Māori culture](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_culture "Māori culture"). In 1642, the Dutch explorer [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman") became the first European to sight and record New Zealand. In 1769 the British explorer [Captain James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") became the first European to set foot on and map New Zealand. In 1840, representatives of the United Kingdom and [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") chiefs signed the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi"), which paved the way for Britain's declaration of sovereignty later that year and the establishment of the Crown [Colony of New Zealand](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand "Colony of New Zealand") in 1841. Subsequently, [a series of conflicts](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars") between the colonial government and [Māori tribes](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") resulted in the [alienation and confiscation](/wiki/New_Zealand_land_confiscations "New Zealand land confiscations") of large amounts of Māori land. New Zealand [became a dominion](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_Zealand "Dominion of New Zealand") in 1907; it [gained full statutory independence](/wiki/Independence_of_New_Zealand "Independence of New Zealand") in 1947, retaining the monarch as [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). Today, the majority of [New Zealand's population](/wiki/New_Zealanders "New Zealanders") of around 5.3 million is of [European descent](/wiki/European_New_Zealanders "European New Zealanders"); the indigenous Māori are the largest minority, followed by [Asians](/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders "Asian New Zealanders") and [Pasifika](/wiki/Pasifika_New_Zealanders "Pasifika New Zealanders"). Reflecting this, [New Zealand's culture](/wiki/Culture_of_New_Zealand "Culture of New Zealand") mainly derives from Māori and early [British](/wiki/British_people "British people") settlers but has recently broadened from increased [immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_New_Zealand "Immigration to New Zealand"). [The official languages](/wiki/Languages_of_New_Zealand "Languages of New Zealand") are English, [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"), and [New Zealand Sign Language](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language "New Zealand Sign Language"), with the [local dialect of English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English") being dominant.

A [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"), New Zealand was the first to introduce a [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage") and to give [women the right to vote](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_New_Zealand "Women's suffrage in New Zealand"). Recognised as a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power"), New Zealand [ranks very highly](/wiki/International_rankings_of_New_Zealand "International rankings of New Zealand") in international measures of [quality of life](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") and [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights_in_New_Zealand "Human rights in New Zealand") and has one of the lowest levels of [perceived corruption](/wiki/Corruption_perception_index "Corruption perception index") in the world. It retains [visible levels of inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality_in_New_Zealand "Economic inequality in New Zealand"), including [structural disparities](/wiki/Structural_discrimination_in_New_Zealand "Structural discrimination in New Zealand") between its Māori and European populations. During the 1980s, New Zealand underwent [major economic changes](/wiki/Rogernomics "Rogernomics") that transformed it from a [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism") to a [liberalised](/wiki/Economic_liberalization "Economic liberalization") [free-trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") economy. The [service sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") dominates the [country's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_New_Zealand "Economy of New Zealand"), followed by the industrial sector, and [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_New_Zealand "Agriculture in New Zealand"); international [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_New_Zealand "Tourism in New Zealand") is also a significant source of revenue. New Zealand and Australia have [a strong relationship](/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93New_Zealand_relations "Australia–New Zealand relations") and are considered to share a strong [Trans-Tasman](/wiki/Trans-Tasman "Trans-Tasman") identity, stemming from centuries of British colonisation. The country is part of [multiple international organisations and forums](/wiki/Outline_of_New_Zealand#International_organisation_membership "Outline of New Zealand").

Nationally, legislative authority is vested in an elected, [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") [Parliament](/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament "New Zealand Parliament"), while executive political power is exercised by the [Government](/wiki/New_Zealand_Government "New Zealand Government"), led by the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand"), currently [Christopher Luxon](/wiki/Christopher_Luxon "Christopher Luxon"). [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") is [the country's king](/wiki/Monarchy_of_New_Zealand "Monarchy of New Zealand") and is represented by the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand"), [Cindy Kiro](/wiki/Cindy_Kiro "Cindy Kiro"). New Zealand is organised into 11 [regional councils](/wiki/Regions_of_New_Zealand "Regions of New Zealand") and 67 [territorial authorities](/wiki/Territorial_authorities_of_New_Zealand "Territorial authorities of New Zealand") for [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Zealand "Local government in New Zealand") purposes. The [Realm of New Zealand](/wiki/Realm_of_New_Zealand "Realm of New Zealand") also includes [Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau "Tokelau") (a [dependent territory](/wiki/Dependent_territory "Dependent territory")); the [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands") and [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue") (self-governing states in [free association](/wiki/Associated_state "Associated state") with New Zealand); and the [Ross Dependency](/wiki/Ross_Dependency "Ross Dependency"), which is New Zealand's [territorial claim in Antarctica](/wiki/Territorial_claims_in_Antarctica "Territorial claims in Antarctica").

## Etymology

Further information: [New Zealand place names](/wiki/New_Zealand_place_names "New Zealand place names")

[![Brown square paper with Dutch writing and a thick red, curved line](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Nova_Zeelandia_by_Janssonius%28134121958%29.jpg/250px-Nova_Zeelandia_by_Janssonius%28134121958%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nova_Zeelandia_by_Janssonius(134121958).jpg)

Detail from a 1666 map showing the western coastline of *Nova Zeelandia* (on this map, north is at the bottom)

The first European visitor to New Zealand, Dutch explorer [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman"), named the islands *Staten Land*, believing they were part of the *[Staten Landt](/wiki/Isla_de_los_Estados "Isla de los Estados")* that [Jacob Le Maire](/wiki/Jacob_Le_Maire "Jacob Le Maire") had sighted off the southern end of South America.[[15]](#cite_note-StatenLandt-23)[[16]](#cite_note-24) The next year, in 1643, [Hendrik Brouwer](/wiki/Hendrik_Brouwer "Hendrik Brouwer") proved that the South American land was just a small island, and Dutch [cartographers](/wiki/Cartographers "Cartographers") subsequently renamed Tasman's discovery *Nova Zeelandia* from [Latin](/wiki/Neo-Latin "Neo-Latin"), after the [Dutch province](/wiki/County_of_Zeeland "County of Zeeland") of [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland").[[15]](#cite_note-StatenLandt-23)[[17]](#cite_note-25) This name was later [anglicised](/wiki/Anglicisation "Anglicisation") to *New Zealand*.[[18]](#cite_note-26)[[19]](#cite_note-Names-27)

This was written as *Nu Tireni* in the [Māori language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") (spelled *Nu Tirani* in Te Tiriti o Waitangi). In 1834, a document written in Māori, "*He Wakaputanga o te Rangatiratanga o Nu Tireni*", was translated into English and became the [Declaration of the Independence of New Zealand](/wiki/Declaration_of_the_Independence_of_New_Zealand "Declaration of the Independence of New Zealand"). It was prepared by *Te W(h)akaminenga o Nga Rangatiratanga o Nga Hapu o Nu Tireni*, the [United Tribes of New Zealand](/wiki/United_Tribes_of_New_Zealand "United Tribes of New Zealand"), and a copy was sent to King [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV"), who had already acknowledged the flag of the United Tribes of New Zealand and who recognised the declaration in a letter from [Lord Glenelg](/wiki/Charles_Grant,_1st_Baron_Glenelg "Charles Grant, 1st Baron Glenelg").[[20]](#cite_note-glenelgletter-28)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer200841-29)

*[Aotearoa](/wiki/Aotearoa "Aotearoa")* (pronounced Māori pronunciation: [[aɔˈtɛaɾɔa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Aotearoa_Pronunciation.wav "File:Aotearoa Pronunciation.wav") in Māori and [/ˌaʊtɛəˈroʊ.ə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") in English; often translated as 'land of the long white cloud')[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKing200341-30) is the current Māori name for New Zealand. It is unknown whether Māori had a name for the whole country before the arrival of Europeans; *Aotearoa* originally referred to just the [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayMaclaganGordon200872-31) Māori had several traditional names for the two main islands, including *Te Ika-a-Māui* ('the fish of [Māui](/wiki/M%C4%81ui_(M%C4%81ori_mythology) "Māui (Māori mythology)")') for the North Island and *Te Waipounamu* ('the waters of [greenstone](/wiki/Pounamu "Pounamu")') or *Te Waka o Aoraki* ('the canoe of [Aoraki](/wiki/Aoraki "Aoraki")') for the [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMein_Smith20056-32) Early European maps labelled the islands North (North Island), Middle (South Island), and South ([Stewart Island / Rakiura](/wiki/Stewart_Island_/_Rakiura "Stewart Island / Rakiura")).[[25]](#cite_note-33)

In 1830, mapmakers began to use "North" and "South" on their maps to distinguish the two largest islands, and by 1907, this was the accepted norm.[[19]](#cite_note-Names-27) The [New Zealand Geographic Board](/wiki/New_Zealand_Geographic_Board "New Zealand Geographic Board") discovered in 2009 that the names of the North Island and South Island had never been formalised, and names and alternative names were formalised in 2013. This set the names as *North Island* or *Te Ika-a-Māui*, and *South Island* or *Te Waipounamu*.[[26]](#cite_note-Williamson2013-34) For each island, either its English or Māori name can be used, or both can be used together.[[26]](#cite_note-Williamson2013-34) Similarly the Māori and English names for the whole country are sometimes used together (*Aotearoa New Zealand*);[[27]](#cite_note-35)[[28]](#cite_note-36) however, this has no official recognition.[[29]](#cite_note-37)

## History

Main article: [History of New Zealand](/wiki/History_of_New_Zealand "History of New Zealand")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of New Zealand history](/wiki/Timeline_of_New_Zealand_history "Timeline of New Zealand history").

[![One set of arrows point from Taiwan to Melanesia to Fiji/Samoa and then to the Marquesas Islands. The population then spread, some going south to New Zealand and others going north to Hawai'i. A second set start in southern Asia and end in Melanesia.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Polynesian_Migration.svg/250px-Polynesian_Migration.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Polynesian_Migration.svg)

The [Māori people](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") descend from [Polynesians](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") whose ancestors emigrated from [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") to [Melanesia](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia") between 3000 and 1000 [BCE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era") and then travelled east, reaching the [Society Islands](/wiki/Society_Islands "Society Islands") c. 1000 CE. After a pause of 200 to 300 years, a new wave of exploration led to the discovery and settlement of New Zealand.[[30]](#cite_note-38)[[31]](#cite_note-39)[[32]](#cite_note-40)

The first people to reach New Zealand were Polynesians in oceangoing *[waka](/wiki/Waka_(canoe) "Waka (canoe)")*, who are believed to have arrived in several waves between about 1280 and 1350. According to most [Māori oral traditions](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_history "Māori history"), the islands were first discovered by the semi-legendary explorer [Kupe](/wiki/Kupe "Kupe") while in pursuit of a giant octopus.[[33]](#cite_note-41) These traditions hold that Kupe was followed by a great fleet of settlers, who set out from Hawaiki in eastern Polynesia around 1350.[[34]](#cite_note-42) The existence of a single great fleet that settled New Zealand has since been superseded by the belief that the majority of settlement was a planned and deliberate event that occurred over several decades.[[35]](#cite_note-43)[[36]](#cite_note-44)[[37]](#cite_note-45)[[38]](#cite_note-46)[[39]](#cite_note-47)

The exact date of this settlement is unclear, with recent sources favouring settlement in the 14th century. While [mitochondrial DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") variability within Māori populations suggest that New Zealand was first settled between 1250 and 1300,[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMein_Smith20056-32)[[40]](#cite_note-48)[[41]](#cite_note-49) no human remains, artefacts or structures can be reliably dated to earlier than the Kaharoa eruption of [Mount Tarawera](/wiki/Mount_Tarawera "Mount Tarawera") around 1314.[[42]](#cite_note-50) This scenario is also consistent with a debated third line of oral evidence,[[43]](#cite_note-Moon_2013-51) traditional genealogies (*[whakapapa](/wiki/Whakapapa "Whakapapa")*) that point to around 1350 as a probable arrival date for several of the [migratory waka](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_migration_canoes "Māori migration canoes") (canoes) from which many Māori trace their descent.[[44]](#cite_note-52)[[45]](#cite_note-53) Some Māori later migrated to the [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands"), where they developed their distinct [Moriori](/wiki/Moriori "Moriori") culture;[[46]](#cite_note-54) a later 1835 invasion by Māori [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") resulted in the massacre and virtual extinction of the Moriori.[[47]](#cite_note-55)

In a hostile 1642 encounter between [Ngāti Tūmatakōkiri](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_T%C5%ABmatak%C5%8Dkiri "Ngāti Tūmatakōkiri") and [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman")'s crew,[[48]](#cite_note-56)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMein_Smith200523-57) four of Tasman's crew members were killed and at least one Māori was hit by [canister shot](/wiki/Canister_shot "Canister shot").[[50]](#cite_note-58) Europeans did not revisit New Zealand until 1769, when British explorer [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") mapped almost the entire coastline.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMein_Smith200523-57) Following Cook, New Zealand was visited by numerous European and North American [whaling](/wiki/History_of_whaling "History of whaling"), [sealing](/wiki/Seal_hunting "Seal hunting"), and trading ships. They traded European food, metal tools, weapons, and other goods for timber, Māori food, artefacts, and water.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKing2003122-59)

The introduction of the [potato](/wiki/Potato "Potato") and the [musket](/wiki/Musket "Musket") transformed Māori agriculture and warfare. Potatoes provided a reliable food surplus, which enabled longer and more sustained military campaigns.[[52]](#cite_note-60) The resulting intertribal [Musket Wars](/wiki/Musket_Wars "Musket Wars") encompassed over 600 battles between 1801 and 1840, killing 30,000–40,000 Māori.[[53]](#cite_note-61) From the early 19th century, Christian [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") began to settle New Zealand, eventually [converting](/wiki/Religious_conversion "Religious conversion") most of the Māori population.[[54]](#cite_note-62) The Māori population declined to around 40% of its pre-contact level during the 19th century; introduced diseases were the major factor.[[55]](#cite_note-63)

[![A torn sheet of paper](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Treatyofwaitangi.jpg/250px-Treatyofwaitangi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treatyofwaitangi.jpg)

The Waitangi sheet from the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi")

The [British Government](/wiki/Resident_minister "Resident minister") appointed [James Busby](/wiki/James_Busby "James Busby") as British Resident to New Zealand in 1832.[[56]](#cite_note-Busby-64) His duties, given to him by Governor Bourke in Sydney, were to protect settlers and traders "of good standing", prevent "outrages" against Māori, and apprehend escaped convicts.[[56]](#cite_note-Busby-64)[[57]](#cite_note-65) In 1835, following an announcement of impending French settlement by [Charles de Thierry](/wiki/Charles_de_Thierry "Charles de Thierry"), the nebulous [United Tribes of New Zealand](/wiki/United_Tribes_of_New_Zealand "United Tribes of New Zealand") sent [a Declaration of Independence](/wiki/He_Whakaputanga "He Whakaputanga") to King [William IV of the United Kingdom](/wiki/William_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "William IV of the United Kingdom") asking for protection.[[56]](#cite_note-Busby-64) The [Colonial Office](/wiki/Colonial_Office "Colonial Office") sent Captain [William Hobson](/wiki/William_Hobson "William Hobson") to claim British sovereignty and negotiate a treaty with the Māori, prompted by growing unrest, the [New Zealand Company](/wiki/New_Zealand_Company "New Zealand Company")'s land-buying survey, and the legal ambiguity of the 1835 Declaration of Independence.[[58]](#cite_note-66) The [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") was first signed in the [Bay of Islands](/wiki/Bay_of_Islands "Bay of Islands") on 6 February 1840.[[59]](#cite_note-Wilson2009-67) In response to the New Zealand Company's attempts to establish an independent settlement in [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington"),[[60]](#cite_note-68)[[61]](#cite_note-69) Hobson declared British sovereignty over all of New Zealand on 21 May 1840, even though copies of the treaty were still circulating throughout the country for Māori to sign.[[62]](#cite_note-70) With the signing of the treaty and declaration of sovereignty, the number of immigrants, particularly from the United Kingdom, began to increase.[[63]](#cite_note-71)

New Zealand was administered as a dependency of the [Colony of New South Wales](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales") until becoming a separate [Crown colony](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony"), the [Colony of New Zealand](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand "Colony of New Zealand"), on 3 May 1841.[[64]](#cite_note-72)[[65]](#cite_note-73) Armed conflict began between the colonial government and Māori in 1843 with the [Wairau Affray](/wiki/Wairau_Affray "Wairau Affray") over land and disagreements over sovereignty. These conflicts, mainly in the North Island, saw thousands of imperial troops and the Royal Navy come to New Zealand and became known as the [New Zealand Wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars"). Following these armed conflicts, large areas of [Māori land were confiscated by the government](/wiki/New_Zealand_land_confiscations "New Zealand land confiscations").[[66]](#cite_note-74)

[![Black and white engraving depicting a crowd of people](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/1863_Meeting_of_Settlers_and_Maoris_at_Hawke%27s_Bay%2C_New_Zealand.jpg/330px-1863_Meeting_of_Settlers_and_Maoris_at_Hawke%27s_Bay%2C_New_Zealand.jpg)](/wiki/File:1863_Meeting_of_Settlers_and_Maoris_at_Hawke%27s_Bay,_New_Zealand.jpg)

A meeting of European and Māori residents of [Hawke's Bay Province](/wiki/Hawke%27s_Bay_Province "Hawke's Bay Province"). Engraving, 1863.

The colony gained a [representative government in 1852](/wiki/New_Zealand_Constitution_Act_1852 "New Zealand Constitution Act 1852"), and the [first Parliament](/wiki/1st_New_Zealand_Parliament "1st New Zealand Parliament") met in 1854.[[67]](#cite_note-G_and_N-75) In 1856 the colony effectively became self-governing, gaining responsibility over all domestic matters, except [native](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") policy, which was granted in the mid-1860s.[[67]](#cite_note-G_and_N-75) Following concerns that the South Island might form a separate colony, premier [Alfred Domett](/wiki/Alfred_Domett "Alfred Domett") moved a resolution to transfer the [capital](/wiki/Capital_of_New_Zealand "Capital of New Zealand") from Auckland to a locality near [Cook Strait](/wiki/Cook_Strait "Cook Strait").[[68]](#cite_note-76)[[69]](#cite_note-77) Wellington was chosen for its central location, with Parliament officially sitting there for the first time in 1865.[[70]](#cite_note-78)

In 1886, New Zealand annexed the volcanic [Kermadec Islands](/wiki/Kermadec_Islands "Kermadec Islands"), about 1,000 km (620 mi) northeast of Auckland. Since 1937, the islands are uninhabited except for about six people at [Raoul Island](/wiki/Raoul_Island "Raoul Island") station. These islands put the northern border of New Zealand at 29 degrees South latitude.[[71]](#cite_note-ENZ1966-79) After the 1982 [UNCLOS](/wiki/UNCLOS "UNCLOS"), the islands contributed significantly to New Zealand's [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone_of_New_Zealand "Exclusive economic zone of New Zealand").[[72]](#cite_note-80)

In 1891, the [Liberal Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Liberal_Party "New Zealand Liberal Party") came to power as the first organised political party.[[73]](#cite_note-Liberal-81) The [Liberal Government](/wiki/Liberal_Government_of_New_Zealand "Liberal Government of New Zealand"), led by [Richard Seddon](/wiki/Richard_Seddon "Richard Seddon") for most of its period in office,[[74]](#cite_note-82) passed many important social and economic measures. In 1893, New Zealand was the first nation in the world to grant all [women the right to vote](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_New_Zealand "Women's suffrage in New Zealand")[[73]](#cite_note-Liberal-81) and pioneered the [adoption of compulsory arbitration](/wiki/Industrial_Conciliation_and_Arbitration_Act_1894 "Industrial Conciliation and Arbitration Act 1894") between employers and unions in 1894.[[75]](#cite_note-83) The Liberals also guaranteed a minimum wage in 1894, a world first.[[76]](#cite_note-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Armistice_Day_Celebrations_at_Municipal_Chambers%2C_1918_%2814666650935%29.jpg/250px-Armistice_Day_Celebrations_at_Municipal_Chambers%2C_1918_%2814666650935%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Armistice_Day_Celebrations_at_Municipal_Chambers,_1918_(14666650935).jpg)

Residents of [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin") celebrate the news of the [Armistice of 11 November 1918](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918").

In 1907, at the request of the New Zealand Parliament, King [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") proclaimed New Zealand a [Dominion](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_Zealand "Dominion of New Zealand") within the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"),[[77]](#cite_note-85) reflecting its self-governing status.[[78]](#cite_note-86) In 1947, New Zealand [adopted](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_Adoption_Act_1947 "Statute of Westminster Adoption Act 1947") the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"), confirming that the [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") could no longer legislate for the country without its consent. The British government's residual legislative powers were later removed by the [Constitution Act 1986](/wiki/Constitution_Act_1986 "Constitution Act 1986"), and final rights of appeal to British courts were abolished in 2003.[[67]](#cite_note-G_and_N-75)

Early in the 20th century, New Zealand was involved in world affairs, fighting in the [First](/wiki/Military_history_of_New_Zealand_during_World_War_I "Military history of New Zealand during World War I") and [Second World Wars](/wiki/Military_history_of_New_Zealand_during_World_War_II "Military history of New Zealand during World War II")[[79]](#cite_note-87) and suffering through the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[80]](#cite_note-88) The depression led to the election of the [first Labour Government](/wiki/First_Labour_Government_of_New_Zealand "First Labour Government of New Zealand") and the establishment of a comprehensive [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") and a [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionist "Protectionist") economy.[[81]](#cite_note-89) New Zealand experienced increasing prosperity following the Second World War,[[82]](#cite_note-90) and Māori began to leave their traditional rural life and move to the cities in search of work.[[83]](#cite_note-91) A [Māori protest movement](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_protest_movement "Māori protest movement") developed, which criticised [Eurocentrism](/wiki/Eurocentrism "Eurocentrism") and worked for greater recognition of [Māori culture](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_culture "Māori culture") and of the Treaty of Waitangi.[[84]](#cite_note-92) In 1975, the [Waitangi Tribunal](/wiki/Waitangi_Tribunal "Waitangi Tribunal") was set up to investigate alleged breaches of the Treaty, and it was enabled to investigate historic grievances in 1985.[[59]](#cite_note-Wilson2009-67) The government has negotiated [settlements of these grievances](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi_claims_and_settlements "Treaty of Waitangi claims and settlements") with many iwi,[[85]](#cite_note-93) although [Māori claims to the foreshore and seabed](/wiki/New_Zealand_foreshore_and_seabed_controversy "New Zealand foreshore and seabed controversy") proved controversial in the 2000s.[[86]](#cite_note-94)[[87]](#cite_note-95)

## Geography and environment

Main articles: [Geography of New Zealand](/wiki/Geography_of_New_Zealand "Geography of New Zealand") and [Environment of New Zealand](/wiki/Environment_of_New_Zealand "Environment of New Zealand")

[![Islands of New Zealand as seen from satellite](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/New_Zealand_23_October_2002.jpg/250px-New_Zealand_23_October_2002.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Zealand_23_October_2002.jpg)

The snow-capped [Southern Alps](/wiki/Southern_Alps "Southern Alps") dominate the South Island, while the North Island's [Northland Peninsula](/wiki/Northland_Peninsula "Northland Peninsula") stretches towards the subtropics.

New Zealand is near the centre of the [water hemisphere](/wiki/Water_hemisphere "Water hemisphere") and is made up of two main islands and more than 700 [smaller islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_New_Zealand "List of islands of New Zealand").[[88]](#cite_note-Walrond1-96) The two main islands (the [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island"), or *Te Ika-a-Māui*, and the [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island"), or *Te Waipounamu*) are separated by [Cook Strait](/wiki/Cook_Strait "Cook Strait"), 22 kilometres (14 mi) wide at its narrowest point.[[89]](#cite_note-97) Besides the North and South Islands, the five largest inhabited islands are [Stewart Island](/wiki/Stewart_Island "Stewart Island") (across the [Foveaux Strait](/wiki/Foveaux_Strait "Foveaux Strait")), [Chatham Island](/wiki/Chatham_Island "Chatham Island"), [Great Barrier Island](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Island "Great Barrier Island") (in the [Hauraki Gulf](/wiki/Hauraki_Gulf "Hauraki Gulf")),[[90]](#cite_note-98) [D'Urville Island](/wiki/D%27Urville_Island_(New_Zealand) "D'Urville Island (New Zealand)") (in the [Marlborough Sounds](/wiki/Marlborough_Sounds "Marlborough Sounds"))[[91]](#cite_note-99) and [Waiheke Island](/wiki/Waiheke_Island "Waiheke Island") (about 22 km (14 mi) from central Auckland).[[92]](#cite_note-100) The nearest continent is [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") to the west north west, with the shortest distance between the Australian and New Zealand mainlands being roughly 1,487 kilometers (924 miles) across the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea"), specifically between [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") and [Fiordland](/wiki/Fiordland "Fiordland"). The closest point between any territory of both countries is between [Macquarie Island](/wiki/Macquarie_Island "Macquarie Island") (Australia) and [Auckland Island](/wiki/Auckland_Island "Auckland Island") (New Zealand), which are about 618 kilometers (384 miles) apart.

New Zealand is long and narrow—over 1,600 kilometres (990 mi) along its north-north-east axis with a maximum width of 400 kilometres (250 mi)[[93]](#cite_note-101)—with about 15,000 km (9,300 mi) of coastline[[94]](#cite_note-CIA-102) and a total land area of 268,000 square kilometres (103,500 sq mi).[[95]](#cite_note-103) Because of its far-flung outlying islands and long coastline, the country has extensive marine resources. Its [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone_of_New_Zealand "Exclusive economic zone of New Zealand") is one of the world's largest, covering more than 15 times its land area.[[96]](#cite_note-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/NZL-mtcook-peters-lookout.jpg/250px-NZL-mtcook-peters-lookout.jpg)](/wiki/File:NZL-mtcook-peters-lookout.jpg)

[Aoraki / Mount Cook](/wiki/Aoraki_/_Mount_Cook "Aoraki / Mount Cook") is the highest point in New Zealand, at 3,724 metres.

The South Island is the largest landmass of New Zealand. It is divided along its length by the [Southern Alps](/wiki/Southern_Alps "Southern Alps").[[97]](#cite_note-105) There are 18 peaks over 3,000 metres (9,800 ft), the highest of which is [Aoraki / Mount Cook](/wiki/Aoraki_/_Mount_Cook "Aoraki / Mount Cook") at 3,724 metres (12,218 ft).[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarden200552-106) [Fiordland](/wiki/Fiordland "Fiordland")'s steep mountains and deep [fiords](/wiki/List_of_fiords_of_New_Zealand "List of fiords of New Zealand") record the extensive ice age glaciation of this southwestern corner of the South Island.[[99]](#cite_note-107) The North Island is less mountainous but is [marked by volcanism](/wiki/Volcanology_of_New_Zealand "Volcanology of New Zealand").[[100]](#cite_note-108) The highly active [Taupō Volcanic Zone](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D_Volcanic_Zone "Taupō Volcanic Zone") has formed a large [volcanic plateau](/wiki/North_Island_Volcanic_Plateau "North Island Volcanic Plateau"), punctuated by the North Island's highest mountain, [Mount Ruapehu](/wiki/Mount_Ruapehu "Mount Ruapehu") (2,797 metres (9,177 ft)). The plateau also hosts the country's largest lake, [Lake Taupō](/wiki/Lake_Taup%C5%8D "Lake Taupō"),[[88]](#cite_note-Walrond1-96) nestled in the [caldera](/wiki/Caldera "Caldera") of one of the world's most active [supervolcanoes](/wiki/Supervolcano "Supervolcano").[[101]](#cite_note-109) New Zealand is prone to [earthquakes](/wiki/List_of_earthquakes_in_New_Zealand "List of earthquakes in New Zealand").

The country owes its varied topography, and perhaps even its emergence above the waves, to the dynamic boundary it straddles between the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Plate "Pacific Plate") and [Indo-Australian Plates](/wiki/Indo-Australian_Plate "Indo-Australian Plate").[[102]](#cite_note-Keith_2009-110) New Zealand is part of [Zealandia](/wiki/Zealandia "Zealandia"), a [microcontinent](/wiki/Microcontinent "Microcontinent") nearly half the size of Australia that gradually submerged after breaking away from the [Gondwanan](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") supercontinent.[[103]](#cite_note-111)[[104]](#cite_note-112) About 25 million years ago, a shift in [plate tectonic](/wiki/Plate_tectonic "Plate tectonic") movements began to [contort and crumple](/wiki/Kaikoura_orogeny "Kaikoura orogeny") the region. This is now most evident in the Southern Alps, formed by [compression of the crust](/wiki/Continental_collision "Continental collision") beside the [Alpine Fault](/wiki/Alpine_Fault "Alpine Fault"). Elsewhere, the plate boundary involves the [subduction](/wiki/Subduction "Subduction") of one plate under the other, producing the [Puysegur Trench](/wiki/Puysegur_Trench "Puysegur Trench") to the south, the [Hikurangi Trough](/wiki/Hikurangi_Trough "Hikurangi Trough") east of the North Island, and the [Kermadec](/wiki/Kermadec_Trench "Kermadec Trench") and [Tonga Trenches](/wiki/Tonga_Trench "Tonga Trench")[[105]](#cite_note-113) further north.[[102]](#cite_note-Keith_2009-110)

New Zealand, together with Australia, is part of a wider region known as [Australasia](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia").[[106]](#cite_note-NZOD-114) It also forms the southwestern extremity of the geographic and ethnographic region called [Polynesia](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia").[[107]](#cite_note-115) Oceania is a wider region encompassing the [Australian continent](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)"), New Zealand, and various island countries in the Pacific Ocean that are not included in the [seven-continent](/wiki/Continent "Continent") model.[[108]](#cite_note-116)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of New Zealand](/wiki/Climate_of_New_Zealand "Climate of New Zealand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/New_Zealand_K%C3%B6ppen.png/250px-New_Zealand_K%C3%B6ppen.png)](/wiki/File:New_Zealand_K%C3%B6ppen.png)

[Köppen-Geiger climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen-Geiger_climate_classification "Köppen-Geiger climate classification") map of New Zealand

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Christchurch_Botanic_Gardens_in_autumn.jpg/250px-Christchurch_Botanic_Gardens_in_autumn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christchurch_Botanic_Gardens_in_autumn.jpg)

Autumn in the [Christchurch Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Christchurch_Botanic_Gardens "Christchurch Botanic Gardens")

New Zealand's climate is predominantly temperate [maritime](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): Cfb), with mean annual temperatures ranging from 10 °C (50 °F) in the south to 16 °C (61 °F) in the north.[[109]](#cite_note-Mullan2009-117) Historical [maxima and minima](/wiki/Maxima_and_minima "Maxima and minima") are 42.4 °C (108.32 °F) in [Rangiora](/wiki/Rangiora "Rangiora"), [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_Region "Canterbury Region") and −25.6 °C (−14.08 °F) in [Ranfurly](/wiki/Ranfurly,_New_Zealand "Ranfurly, New Zealand"), [Otago](/wiki/Otago "Otago").[[110]](#cite_note-niwa-118) Conditions vary sharply across regions, from extremely wet on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast,_New_Zealand "West Coast, New Zealand") of the South Island to [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") in [Central Otago](/wiki/Central_Otago "Central Otago") and the [Mackenzie Basin](/wiki/Mackenzie_Basin "Mackenzie Basin") of inland Canterbury and [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical "Subtropical") in [Northland](/wiki/Northland_Peninsula "Northland Peninsula").[[111]](#cite_note-119)[[112]](#cite_note-120)

Of the seven largest cities, [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch") is the driest, receiving on average only 618 millimetres (24.3 in) of rain per year. Wellington is the wettest, receiving almost twice that amount.[[113]](#cite_note-121) Auckland, Wellington and Christchurch all receive a yearly average of more than 2,000 hours of sunshine. The southern and southwestern parts of the South Island have a cooler and cloudier climate, with around 1,400–1,600 hours. The northern and northeastern parts of the South Island are the sunniest areas of the country and receive about 2,400–2,500 hours.[[114]](#cite_note-122)

The general snow season is early June until early October, though [cold snaps](/wiki/Cold_snap "Cold snap") can occur outside this season.[[115]](#cite_note-123) Snowfall is common in the eastern and southern parts of the South Island and mountain areas across the country.[[109]](#cite_note-Mullan2009-117)
